      Performance fees
   =20
 =20

My crude model of hedge fund management is:
=09You=E2=80=99ll have good times and bad times.
  [1]

=09In the good times, investors will rush to put their money into=C2=A0your=
 fund, and will do anything to get you to take their money. In the bad time=
s, they=E2=80=99ll rush to get out, and they=E2=80=99ll do anything to get =
their money back.=09You should spend the good times preparing for the bad t=
imes: In the good times, when investors say =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99ll do anyth=
ing to get you to take our money,=E2=80=9D you reply =E2=80=9Cokay sure no =
problem here=E2=80=99s a contract locking your money up for 10 years.=E2=80=
=9D And then in the bad times, when investors say =E2=80=9Cwe want=C2=A0our=
 money back,=E2=80=9D you reply =E2=80=9Chahahaha nice try, remember that l=
ockup?=E2=80=9D

Obviously your investors will grumble, when you hand them that 10-year lock=
up in the good times, but if your investors aren=E2=80=99t grumbling in the=
 good times then you are leaving money on the table. Similarly your investo=
rs will=C2=A0grumble, in the bad times, when they are stuck with that locku=
p, but you would much rather have them=C2=A0grumbling=C2=A0than=C2=A0taking=
 their money back. =E2=80=9CIf your investors are complaining to the press =
about how onerous your liquidity terms are,=E2=80=9D=C2=A0I once wrote,=C2=
=A0=E2=80=9Cyou made the right call on the liquidity terms.=E2=80=9D
These are very good times for large multi-strategy hedge funds, so it=E2=80=
=99s   time to turn the screws:

Izzy Englander=E2=80=99s Millennium Management has changed its terms to ens=
ure that clients always pay a minimum fee, even if the hedge fund loses mon=
ey. =C2=A0=C2=A0
They will now pay annual fees of about 1% of assets or 20% of investment ga=
ins =E2=80=94 whichever is greater, according to a client letter seen by Bl=
oomberg. It=E2=80=99s part of an effort to =E2=80=9Creflect current industr=
y-standard approaches=E2=80=9D adopted by other multi-strategy peers, Mille=
nnium said in the Feb. 21 letter. =E2=80=A6
Millennium, like most of its competitors, also passes along to clients expe=
nses that include portfolio-manager compensation and legal and accounting c=
osts. =E2=80=A6
The new terms align with Millennium=E2=80=99s broader push to create a stab=
le business that=E2=80=99s unencumbered by frequent redemptions or big redu=
ctions in annual income. The firm recently completed a plan to lock up clie=
nt capital for longer, extending the full redemption period to five years f=
rom one. Its executives view reliable capital as a key tool to attract and =
retain talent and invest in technology and infrastructure, among other thin=
gs. =C2=A0

Yes reliable capital is extremely good for a hedge fund! The time to make i=
t reliable is when it wants in.
This structure of, like, 1-or-20 fees=C2=A0sounds=C2=A0lower than the tradi=
tional hedge fund fee structure of 2-and-20, that is, a 2% fee on assets=C2=
=A0plus=C2=A020% of gains, but the   =E2=80=9Cpass-through=E2=80=9D fees fo=
r =E2=80=9Cexpenses that include portfolio-manager compensation=E2=80=9D  a=
ctually make=C2=A0it higher. =E2=80=9CThe multi-manager fund is  more like =
7-and-20,=E2=80=9D though the pass-through fees vary by fund and year. The =
point is that Millennium charges its costs (including pay), and then takes =
20% of the profits, but now that performance fee is floored at 1% of assets=
 even if the profits are negative.=C2=A0
I suppose in some theoretical sense, as an investor, you should want your h=
edge fund manager to have =E2=80=9Cbig reductions in annual income=E2=80=9D=
 when=C2=A0 performance is bad? Originally, that sort of incentive alignmen=
t was kind of the point of the hedge fund model. And yet! As an investor, y=
ou should also want to invest with the best hedge fund managers. And the be=
st hedge fund managers are smart enough to make sure they make money no mat=
ter what.

Speaking of which, yesterday Institutional Investor  published its annual =
=E2=80=9CRich List=E2=80=9D of =E2=80=9Cthe 25 highest-earning hedge fund m=
anagers, based on a combination of fee earnings and gains on their own capi=
tal.=E2=80=9D Bloomberg=E2=80=99s own   high earners list came out last mon=
th.
Both lists include Said Haidar of Haidar Capital, who seems to be=C2=A0an o=
ld-school guy who makes=C2=A0huge leveraged bets=C2=A0and eats what he kill=
s; last year he killed it and=C2=A0his fund was up 193%, good for the sixth=
 spot on Bloomberg=E2=80=99s list and the tenth on II=E2=80=99s. He =E2=80=
=9Cruns his high-conviction macro strategy with abundant leverage, which le=
ads to double-digit gains but also losses,=E2=80=9D and   as of last month =
he was =E2=80=9Coff to a poor start in 2023.=E2=80=9D I   wrote about him l=
ast month: =E2=80=9CReally the best-performing hedge fund each year =E2=80=
=94 as measured by percentage returns =E2=80=94 always should be one that r=
uns at 50x leverage. =E2=80=A6 Obviously the worst-performing hedge fund ea=
ch year should be a different one that runs at 50x leverage.=E2=80=9D Eat w=
hat you kill, also sometimes get killed.
But the top spots on these lists are mostly the big institutionalized multi=
-manager funds: Citadel, Millennium and Point72 top both rankings. It is a =
different model: Instead of trusting your money to the gut instincts of a c=
harismatic genius who takes huge swings, you trust your money to an alpha f=
actory full of anonymous portfolio managers whose performance is ruthlessly=
 monitored, whose exposures to market factors are=C2=A0carefully tracked, a=
nd who are fired if they lose too much money. And you pay them, not just a =
percentage of the profits that they make for you, but also big steady fees =
to pay for all of that institutional structure.
The bet here is basically that all that institutional structure is valuable=
, that it provides something that investors want.
  [2]
=C2=A0If the thing that you want, as an investor, is a charismatic genius m=
anager who works really hard to find high-conviction=C2=A0trades, then you =
should want incentive alignment, performance-based fees and the occasional =
193% return when that manager gets it exactly right. If the thing that you =
want is steady returns that are uncorrelated to the broader market, then th=
e way to pay for it might be with large fees that are uncorrelated to perfo=
rmance.
I mean, not really uncorrelated;=C2=A0Ken Griffin, Izzy Englander and Steve=
 Cohen had very good years for themselves because their funds had very good=
 years for their clients. But for a hedge fund manager, the best long-term =
way to=C2=A0use=C2=A0that performance might be to transform your fund into =
an institution.

   =20
      10b5-1 abuse
   =20
 =20

The problem is that if you are a senior executive at a public company, you =
are constantly learning things that could be material to its stock price. Y=
ou look at weekly sales numbers, which give you a picture of how the quarte=
r will develop. You meet with customers and=C2=A0they say things like =E2=
=80=9Cwe love your service and want to deepen our relationship=E2=80=9D or =
=E2=80=9Cwe are annoyed and looking for alternatives, how about a price cut=
.=E2=80=9D If you are a top executive, everything that you do is material t=
o the business; if it wasn=E2=80=99t material to the business, someone else=
 would do it.
In a sense this means that any time you buy or sell your company=E2=80=99s =
stock, you are =E2=80=9Cinsider trading,=E2=80=9D trading while in possessi=
on of material nonpublic information. There are solutions to this problem. =
One is the concept of the =E2=80=9Copen window=E2=80=9D:=C2=A0A public comp=
any will usually have a rule saying that its employees can only trade the s=
tock in certain open-window periods shortly after the company releases earn=
ings. The theory is that the earnings release =E2=80=94 and the earnings ca=
ll that usually comes with it =E2=80=94 contains all of the material inform=
ation about the company, so everything that executives know is now public, =
so the executives are free to trade.
  [3]

Another important one, in the US,=C2=A0is the 10b5-1 plan, named after a US=
 Securities and Exchange Commission  rule. The idea in a 10b5-1 plan is tha=
t, when an executive does=C2=A0not=C2=A0have material nonpublic information=
,
  [4]
=C2=A0she can enter into an automated plan with her broker telling the brok=
er what trades to make in the future. You announce earnings, you wait a day=
 or two, then you sign a plan with your broker saying =E2=80=9Csell 10,000 =
shares per month for a year,=C2=A0starting next month.=E2=80=9D When your b=
roker actually sells those shares for you, you might=C2=A0have=C2=A0materia=
l nonpublic information, but you are not=C2=A0trading=C2=A0on that informat=
ion: The sales are automated, and you signed up for them before you learned=
 any material nonpublic information.=C2=A0
There are ways to abuse this. A simple way is: On Monday, you learn some re=
ally bad news that isn=E2=80=99t yet public; on Tuesday,=C2=A0you sign a 10=
b5-1 plan saying =E2=80=9Csell everything tomorrow=E2=80=9D;=C2=A0on Wednes=
day, the plan sells all your stock; and on Thursday you announce the bad ne=
ws and the stock tanks. This one is boring. It is not even really an abuse =
of Rule 10b5-1: This is just=C2=A0insider trading, and Rule 10b5-1 explicit=
ly says that you have to enter the plan =E2=80=9Cbefore becoming aware of t=
he information=E2=80=9D for it to work. Still, lots of information is kind =
of ambiguous, and people do worry about this sort of abuse.
In December, the SEC  amended Rule 10b5-1 to address this concern by requir=
ing a =E2=80=9Ccooling-off period=E2=80=9D: If a director or officer of a c=
ompany signs a=C2=A010b5-1 plan, it can=E2=80=99t actually trade any stock =
for 90 days.
  [5]
=C2=A0The idea is that whether or not you know anything material=C2=A0now, =
it will probably be public or stale in three months, so you definitely won=
=E2=80=99t be insider trading when your=C2=A0automatic trades start in thre=
e months.
A more interesting sort of abuse is: On Jan. 1, you have no material nonpub=
lic information. But you know that on Feb. 15, your company will report ear=
nings; you don=E2=80=99t know what they will be, but you know that the resu=
lts are hotly anticipated and will be very important to the market. Also=C2=
=A0you=C2=A0will know the earnings by Feb.=C2=A05, because you are a senior=
 executive and will be involved in preparing the earnings release. So what =
you do is, you adopt a 10b5-1 plan, on Jan. 1, telling your broker =E2=80=
=9Csell all of my stock over a five-day period starting on Feb. 6.=E2=80=9D=
 You have no material nonpublic information and the plan doesn=E2=80=99t st=
art for a month, so you look good. Then on Feb. 5, you find out the earning=
s. If they are good, you call your broker and=C2=A0cancel the plan: You hav=
e material nonpublic information, but you=C2=A0are not insider trading, bec=
ause all you are doing is=C2=A0deciding not to trade. If the earnings are b=
ad, you do nothing, and your broker dumps all your stock before the earning=
s are announced: This is not insider trading, because the sales are all mad=
e under an automated 10b5-1 plan.
This is also probably not allowed: Rule 10b5-1 says that you have to have e=
ntered into your plan =E2=80=9Cin good faith and not as part of a plan or s=
cheme to evade the prohibitions of this section,=E2=80=9D and this scheme p=
retty clearly looks like bad faith. In practice it is not clear how you get=
=C2=A0caught, though: If you end up doing the trade, then it looks like you=
 were acting in good faith (you signed a plan, and then followed it); if yo=
u end up not doing the trade, then you didn=E2=80=99t do any trades, so the=
y can=E2=80=99t really get you for insider trading.
  [6]
=C2=A0(Not legal advice!)
But last week the SEC did bring an insider trading case about alleged 10b5-=
1 abuse. Terren Peizer was the executive chairman of Ontrak Inc., a behavio=
ral-health-care company whose main customers are a handful of big health in=
surance companies. In March 2021, Ontrak announced that its largest custome=
r (called =E2=80=9CCustomer B=E2=80=9D in  the SEC complaint)=C2=A0was term=
inating its contract; the stock fell 46% on the day, and =E2=80=9CPeizer lo=
st over $265 million in the value of his shares in a single day when Ontrak=
 announced the loss of Customer B.=E2=80=9D
By late March, Ontrak=E2=80=99s next-largest customer (called =E2=80=9CCust=
omer A=E2=80=9D in the complaint) was also =E2=80=9Cnot satisfied with Ontr=
ak=E2=80=99s services and considering terminating its contract.=E2=80=9D On=
trak=E2=80=99s chief executive officer and its president=C2=A0tried to salv=
age the relationship, and they kept Peizer =E2=80=94=C2=A0the executive cha=
irman =E2=80=94=C2=A0up to speed:

Throughout April 2021, Peizer communicated with numerous individuals at Ont=
rak about the importance of saving the Customer A relationship. For example=
, on April 3, 2021, Peizer texted an Ontrak consultant and close confidant:=
 =E2=80=9CWe just need to save [Customer A] and we are on our way.=E2=80=9D
On April 14, 2021, Peizer again texted the consultant that Ontrak=E2=80=99s=
 CEO =E2=80=9Cneeds to still save [Customer A]!=E2=80=9D
The=C2=A0next day, Peizer texted Ontrak=E2=80=99s CEO: =E2=80=9CPlease just=
 save [Customer A]=E2=80=A6then we will get back =E2=80=98Ontrak.=E2=80=99=
=E2=80=9D

I wonder how often he used that line. Anyway:
At the same time that Peizer was communicating with others at Ontrak about =
saving the relationship with Customer A, he was pursuing a plan to sell sha=
res of Ontrak securities. On May 3, 2021, Peizer contacted one of his broke=
rage firms (=E2=80=9CBroker 1=E2=80=9D) to set up a purported Rule 10b5-1 p=
lan. Peizer explained in an email: =E2=80=9CI have 685,929 . . . warrants t=
hat expire August 15th-30th. I want to do a plan to sells 10,000 shares a d=
ay (~68 trading days). We report our Q1 numbers Thursday after the close an=
d our window opens up Friday after the close. My intent is to exercise the =
warrants, and file the plan.=E2=80=9D
This was before the SEC amended Rule 10b5-1, so there was no SEC-required c=
ooling-off period. Peizer could have signed the plan and started trading im=
mediately, but his brokerage had its own 30-day cooling-off period to avoid=
 any risk of impropriety. They offered to go down to 14 days, =E2=80=9Cbut =
having no cooling off is not negotiable.=E2=80=9D So he found another broke=
r:

On May 4, 2021, Peizer texted a brokerage employee at Broker 2, =E2=80=9CWh=
en you execute 10b5-1 plans, once the plan is filed, does [your brokerage f=
irm] have a=C2=A0cooling off period?=E2=80=9D The employee responded, =E2=
=80=9CIt has been our experience that the cooling off period is dictated by=
 the company.=E2=80=9D Peizer replied, =E2=80=9Cagreed.=E2=80=9D
Upon determining that Broker 2 did not require a cooling off period, but wo=
uld allow him to start selling stock immediately after establishing a Rule =
10b5-1 plan, Peizer moved forward in setting up a purported Rule 10b5-1 pla=
n at Broker 2. =E2=80=A6
Specifically, the May Trading Plan instructed Broker 2 to sell 11,000 share=
s of Ontrak common stock per day at the volume weighted average price (=E2=
=80=9CVWAP=E2=80=9D) (or more shares per day at specified limit prices), st=
arting on May 11, 2021=E2=80=94the first business day after the May Trading=
 Plan was established.=C2=A0

The timing was pretty good:

On a May 18, 2021 call, Customer A notified Ontrak=E2=80=99s CEO of its int=
ention to terminate its contract effective December 31, 2021.
That same day, Ontrak=E2=80=99s CEO notified Peizer via text message: =E2=
=80=9CCustomer A is intending to end relationship at the end of the year 12=
-31-21=E2=80=A6.They are really firm with me. Decision has been made.=E2=80=
=9D

Nonetheless, Ontrak kept trying to save the relationship, and did not=C2=A0=
announce Customer A=E2=80=99s termination, so the stock stayed up while Pei=
zer=E2=80=99s 10b5-1 plan was selling. On Aug. 13, he did a new 10b5-1 plan=
:

Specifically, the August Trading Plan instructed Broker 2 to sell 11,000 sh=
ares of Ontrak common stock per day at the VWAP (or more shares per day at =
specified limit prices), starting the next trading day, August 16, 2021.
In an internal email between Broker 2 employees, one stated that he =E2=80=
=9Calways recommend[s] 30 day cooling off, but for Terren [Peizer] that won=
=E2=80=99t work.=E2=80=9D

Meanwhile:
On August 13, 2021=E2=80=94the same day that Peizer set up a second Rule 10=
b5-1 trading plan to sell Ontrak stock, as discussed below=E2=80=94Peizer c=
alled the Ontrak Sales Employee and asked if Customer A was going to termin=
ate the contract. The Ontrak Sales Employee indicated to Peizer that he tho=
ught Customer A would terminate.
The timing here was less good: On Aug. 18, Customer A sent Ontrak a formal =
termination, and on Aug. 19 Ontrak  announced the termination publicly. The=
 stock traded down 44% that day, closing at $11.68.=C2=A0Ontrak closed yest=
erday=C2=A0at $0.56, for a market capitalization of about $15 million. Turn=
s out losing all your biggest customers is bad. Peizer sold $19.2 million o=
f stock under his first plan and $1.9 million under his second (in the coup=
le of days before the termination was announced); the SEC says that was ins=
ider trading.
There are, I think, three ways to tell this story:
=09Peizer was involved in the business, and like any senior executive he kn=
ew a bunch of stuff, there was a lot going on, and nothing was certain. Ont=
rak was constantly negotiating with customers and getting feedback; that is=
 just part of the job. In May, he decided to sell some stock from some opti=
ons that were expiring, so he signed a deal to do that. He had no =E2=80=9C=
material nonpublic information=E2=80=9D: He didn=E2=80=99t know that a big =
cancellation was coming; he just knew that the business was hard and risky =
and some customers were complaining. (It=E2=80=99s harder to defend the Aug=
ust trades, but I suppose they were just a continuation of the May ones?)=
=09Peizer had material nonpublic information, in May and in August, that Cu=
stomer A was planning to cancel. So he decided to sell stock as quickly as =
possible, but put the fig leaf of a 10b5-1 plan over his sales. But 10b5-1 =
is not a defense if you knew the material nonpublic information when you si=
gned the plan; the fig leaf doesn=E2=80=99t work.=09Peizer kinda knew stuff=
 was going south, but he wanted to give it time to play out. So he signed a=
 plan to start dumping stock, one that he could have canceled if things imp=
roved with Customer A. But they never did, so he kept selling. He didn=E2=
=80=99t know anything material and bad when he signed the May plan, but he =
signed the May plan to give himself some optionality to sell if he learned =
anything material and bad, and that itself is a kind of insider trading.

      Oh Elon
   =20
 =20

I don't know? Yesterday Elon Musk  got in a Twitter fight with Halli Thorle=
ifsson, Iceland=E2=80=99s  Person of the Year, who apparently lost his job =
at Twitter Inc. in one of Musk=E2=80=99s many rounds of layoffs? Musk=C2=A0=
lost the Twitter fight=C2=A0very, very badly? And=C2=A0made some comments s=
uggesting Thorleifsson=C2=A0was fired due to his disability? And it turned =
out that Thorleifsson sold his company to Twitter and is apparently in line=
 for a huge payout if he was fired? And then Musk=C2=A0un-fired him=C2=A0an=
d =E2=80=A6=C2=A0apologized? Presumably the lawyers who have been chasing h=
im around for the last six months were finally able to tackle him and take =
his phone?=C2=A0
Venture capitalist=C2=A0Parker Thompson tweeted: =E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s cool=
 that Elon is making this site more entertaining by developing cartoon hero=
es and villains for us to invest in emotionally, but if I put in $44b I wou=
ld=E2=80=99ve picked to be one of the heroes.=E2=80=9D I truly do not under=
stand why, if you were the richest person in the world and the chief execut=
ive officer of like five car/rocket/tunnel/brain/etc. companies, you would =
spend your time portraying yourself as a petty jerk on social media! You ca=
n just not do that! You=E2=80=99re free! The point of all that money and po=
wer and accomplishment is that you can choose how to spend your time and at=
tention; you can hire people to do the bad parts of your job; you can focus=
 on what matters to you and stay above petty fights.=C2=A0You get to have a=
 nice life! Why choose to have a bad one?
Elsewhere in Musk, he may have  fired too many people:

The Federal Trade Commission is intensifying an investigation into Twitter=
=E2=80=99s data and privacy practices and is seeking testimony from Elon Mu=
sk, who has laid off the bulk of Twitter=E2=80=99s work force since acquiri=
ng the company last year.
The investigation is focused on whether Twitter has adequate resources to p=
rotect its users=E2=80=99 privacy after the mass layoffs and budget cuts or=
dered by Mr. Musk, said five people familiar with the investigation who spo=
ke on the condition of anonymity.

And:

Elon Musk and the EU are in a dispute over the Twitter owner=E2=80=99s plan=
 to use more volunteers and artificial intelligence to help moderate the so=
cial media platform, as the company responds to strict new rules designed t=
o police online content.
According to four people familiar with talks between Musk, Twitter executiv=
es and regulators in Brussels, the billionaire has been told to hire more h=
uman moderators and fact-checkers to review posts.

When Musk was first musing about buying Twitter last April,   I wrote that =
(1) there would be no real regulatory review of=C2=A0any deal and (2) that =
was kinda weird:
It is not in a heavily regulated industry,=C2=A0and there=E2=80=99s no part=
icular antitrust problem with=C2=A0Musk acquiring it. =E2=80=9CWorld=E2=80=
=99s richest person acquires the main venue for public communication=E2=80=
=9D does=C2=A0seem=C2=A0like the sort of thing that ought to=C2=A0raise reg=
ulatory concerns, but in our actually existing system I=E2=80=99m not sure =
what those concerns=C2=A0would be.=C2=A0
If Musk had been trying to buy, like, a bank, or a casino, some regulator w=
ould probably have asked him questions like =E2=80=9Cdo you plan to fire al=
l your compliance staff,=E2=80=9D and if he had said =E2=80=9Csure do,=E2=
=80=9D the regulator would have blocked the deal. Social media, for all of =
its=C2=A0social and political=C2=A0importance, mostly isn=E2=80=99t an indu=
stry like that; there is no powerful social-media regulator who gets to rev=
iew deals and reject unsuitable owners. But it is not=C2=A0unregulated, the=
re are touch points for regulators, and if you do=C2=A0fire all the complia=
nce people you will eventually get in trouble.
Still elsewhere in the Musk roundup:
=09=E2=80=9CTesla Under Investigation After   Steering Wheels Fall Off Whil=
e Driving.=E2=80=9D=09=E2=80=9CElon Musk Says Twitter Could Be =E2=80=98Cas=
h-Flow Positive=E2=80=99 Within Months.=E2=80=9D

on=E2=80=99t really prohibit terminating the plans, and it=E2=80=99s not cl=
ear how they could. They do require officers to certify that they are =E2=
=80=9Cadopting the plan in good faith and not as part of a plan or scheme t=

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=

5px;">Performance fees</h2>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">My crude model of hedge fund management is:</p=
>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You=E2=80=99ll have good times and ba=
d times.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-colo=
r: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a>
</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">In the good times, investors will=
 rush to put their money into=C2=A0your fund, and will do anything to get y=
ou to take their money. In the bad times, they=E2=80=99ll rush to get out, =
and they=E2=80=99ll do anything to get their money back.</li>=09<li style=
=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You should spend the good times preparing for the =
bad times: In the good times, when investors say =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99ll do =
anything to get you to take our money,=E2=80=9D you reply =E2=80=9Cokay sur=
e no problem here=E2=80=99s a contract locking your money up for 10 years.=
=E2=80=9D And then in the bad times, when investors say =E2=80=9Cwe want=C2=
=A0our money back,=E2=80=9D you reply =E2=80=9Chahahaha nice try, remember =
that lockup?=E2=80=9D</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Obviously your investors will <em>grumble</em>=
, when you hand them that 10-year lockup in the good times, but if your inv=
estors aren=E2=80=99t grumbling in the good times then you are leaving mone=
y on the table. Similarly your investors will=C2=A0<em>grumble</em>, in the=
 bad times, when they are stuck with that lockup, but you would much rather=
 have them=C2=A0grumbling<em>=C2=A0</em>than=C2=A0taking their money back. =
=E2=80=9CIf your investors are complaining to the press about how onerous y=
our liquidity terms are,=E2=80=9D=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombe=
rgbusiness.com/click/30772176.262407/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcG=
luaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMTgtMDctMDUvaXQtcy1iZXR0ZXItdG8tYmUtcmljaC10aGFuLXJpZ=
2h0P2NtcGlkPUJCRDAzMDgyM19NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJj=
ZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIzMDMwOCZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f=
07ceef4b8524a319a23cB63b25a2e" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscop=
e" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !import=
ant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-=
style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">I once wrote</a>,=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cyou =
made the right call on the liquidity terms.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">These are very good times for large multi-stra=
tegy hedge funds, so it=E2=80=99s <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbus=
iness.com/click/30772176.262407/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2F=
ydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDMtMDgvbWlsbGVubml1bS10by1jaGFyZ2UtZmVlcy1ldmVuLXdoZW4tdG=
hlLWhlZGdlLWZ1bmQtbG9zZXMtbW9uZXk_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDMwODIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX=
21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMwMzA4JnV0bV9j=
YW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB6e71cd5e" itemprop=3D"Sto=
ryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; =
text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-c=
olor: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  time =
to turn the screws</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Izzy Englander=E2=80=99s Millennium Management=
 has changed its terms to ensure that clients always pay a minimum fee, eve=
n if the hedge fund loses money. =C2=A0=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">They will now pay annual fees of about 1% of a=
ssets or 20% of investment gains =E2=80=94 whichever is greater, according =
to a client letter seen by Bloomberg. It=E2=80=99s part of an effort to =E2=
=80=9Creflect current industry-standard approaches=E2=80=9D adopted by othe=
r multi-strategy peers, Millennium said in the Feb. 21 letter. =E2=80=A6</p=
>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Millennium, like most of its competitors, also=
 passes along to clients expenses that include portfolio-manager compensati=
on and legal and accounting costs. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The new terms align with Millennium=E2=80=99s =
broader push to create a stable business that=E2=80=99s unencumbered by fre=
quent redemptions or big reductions in annual income. The firm recently com=
pleted a plan to lock up client capital for longer, extending the full rede=
mption period to five years from one. Its executives view reliable capital =
as a key tool to attract and retain talent and invest in technology and inf=
rastructure, among other things. =C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Yes reliable capital is extremely good for a h=
edge fund! The time to make it reliable is when it wants in.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This structure of, like, 1-or-20 fees=C2=A0<em=
>sounds=C2=A0</em>lower than the traditional hedge fund fee structure of 2-=
and-20, that is, a 2% fee on assets=C2=A0<em>plus=C2=A0</em>20% of gains, b=
ut the <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30772176.26=
2407/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMTAtMjYvYn=
JldmFuLWhvd2FyZC10by1jaGFyZ2UtZmVlLXRoYXQtcy1mdWVsaW5nLWhlZGdlLWZ1bmQtdGFsZ=
W50LXdhcj9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMzA4MjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9z=
b3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzAzMDgmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY=
/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBd269030c" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"ite=
mscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !i=
mportant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bo=
ttom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  =E2=80=9Cpass-through=E2=80=
=9D fees</a> for =E2=80=9Cexpenses that include portfolio-manager compensat=
ion=E2=80=9D <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30772=
176.262407/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cudmFsZW5zLXJlc2VhcmNoLmNvbS9pbnZlc3Rvci1lc3NlbnRp=
YWxzLWRhaWx5L21pbGxlbm5pdW0tbWFuYWdlbWVudHMtZnVuZC1vZi1mdW5kcy1hcHByb2FjaC1=
tYWRlLWlzcmFlbC1lbmdsYW5kZXItdGhlLWhpZ2hlc3QtZWFybmluZy1mdW5kLW1hbmFnZXItbG=
FzdC15ZWFyLw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB0605ff6a" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" item=
scope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decorat=
ion: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00000=
0; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> actually</a> make=
=C2=A0it <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30772176.=
262407/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cucGlvbmxpbmUuY29tL2hlZGdlLWZ1bmRzL2JyZXZhbi1ob3dhcmQt=
Y2hhcmdlLXBhc3MtdGhyb3VnaC1mZWVzLXN0b2tpbmctdGFsZW50LXdhcg/607f07ceef4b8524=
a319a23cB400f4be5" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D=
"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-=
bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid=
; background-color: #ccc;">higher</a>. =E2=80=9CThe multi-manager fund is <=
a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30772176.262407/aHR=
0cHM6Ly93d3cucmV1dGVycy5jb20vYnVzaW5lc3MvZmluYW5jZS9tdWx0aS1tYW5hZ2VyLWhlZG=

4b8524a319a23cB1000bc87" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" tar=
get=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; b=
order-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style:=
 solid; background-color: #ccc;"> more like 7-and-20</a>,=E2=80=9D though t=
he pass-through fees vary by fund and year. The point is that Millennium ch=
arges its costs (including pay), and then takes 20% of the profits, but now=
 that performance fee is floored at 1% of assets even if the profits are ne=
gative.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I suppose in some theoretical sense, as an inv=
estor, you should <em>want</em> your hedge fund manager to have =E2=80=9Cbi=
g reductions in annual income=E2=80=9D when=C2=A0 performance is bad? Origi=
nally, that sort of incentive alignment was kind of the point of the hedge =
fund model. And yet! As an investor, you should also want to invest with th=
e best hedge fund managers. And the best hedge fund managers are smart enou=
gh to make sure they make money no matter what.</p>

  high earners list</a> came out last month.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Both lists include Said Haidar of Haidar Capit=
al, who seems to be=C2=A0an old-school guy who makes=C2=A0huge leveraged be=
ts=C2=A0and eats what he kills; last year he killed it and=C2=A0his fund wa=
s up 193%, good for the sixth spot on Bloomberg=E2=80=99s list and the tent=
h on II=E2=80=99s. He =E2=80=9Cruns his high-conviction macro strategy with=
 abundant leverage, which leads to double-digit gains but also losses,=E2=
=80=9D and <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/3077217=
6.262407/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDItMD=
cvaGFpZGFyLXMtbGV2ZXJhZ2VkLWhlZGdlLWZ1bmQtc2lua3MtMTMtYWZ0ZXItcmVjb3JkLTIwM=

b3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzAzMDgmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY=
/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB31035b3e" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"ite=
mscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !i=
mportant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bo=
ttom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  as of last month</a> he was =
=E2=80=9Coff to a poor start in 2023.=E2=80=9D I <a href=3D"https://link.ma=
il.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30772176.262407/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJn=
LmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDItMjEvYW1jLWFwZXMtaGF0ZS1hbWMtcy1hcGV=
zP2NtcGlkPUJCRDAzMDgyM19NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT=

 target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !importan=
t; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-st=
yle: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  wrote about him last month</a>: =E2=
=80=9CReally the best-performing hedge fund each year =E2=80=94 as measured=
 by percentage returns =E2=80=94 always should be one that runs at 50x leve=
rage. =E2=80=A6 Obviously the worst-performing hedge fund each year should =
be a different one that runs at 50x leverage.=E2=80=9D Eat what you kill, a=
lso sometimes get killed.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But the <em>top</em> spots on these lists are =
mostly the big institutionalized multi-manager funds: Citadel, Millennium a=
nd Point72 top both rankings. It is a different model: Instead of trusting =
your money to the gut instincts of a charismatic genius who takes huge swin=
gs, you trust your money to an alpha factory full of anonymous portfolio ma=
nagers whose performance is ruthlessly monitored, whose exposures to market=
 factors are=C2=A0carefully tracked, and who are fired if they lose too muc=
h money. And you pay them, not just a percentage of the profits that they m=
ake for you, but also big steady fees to pay for all of that institutional =
structure.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The bet here is basically that all that instit=
utional structure is valuable, that it provides something that investors wa=
nt.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"color: #000000=
; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom=
-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: tr=
ansparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a>=C2=A0If the thing that you want, as an investor, is a charismatic geni=
us manager who works really hard to find high-conviction=C2=A0trades, then =
you should want incentive alignment, performance-based fees and the occasio=
nal 193% return when that manager gets it exactly right. If the thing that =
you want is steady returns that are uncorrelated to the broader market, the=
n the way to pay for it might be with large fees that are uncorrelated to p=
erformance.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I mean, not really <em>un</em>correlated;=C2=
=A0Ken Griffin, Izzy Englander and Steve Cohen had very good years for them=
selves because their funds had very good years for their clients. But for a=
 hedge fund manager, the best long-term way to=C2=A0<em>use=C2=A0</em>that =
performance might be to transform your fund into an institution.</p>

<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">10b5-1 abuse</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The problem is that if you are a senior execut=
ive at a public company, you are constantly learning things that could be m=
aterial to its stock price. You look at weekly sales numbers, which give yo=
u a picture of how the quarter will develop. You meet with customers and=C2=
=A0they say things like =E2=80=9Cwe love your service and want to deepen ou=
r relationship=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Cwe are annoyed and looking for alternat=
ives, how about a price cut.=E2=80=9D If you are a top executive, everythin=
g that you do is material to the business; if it wasn=E2=80=99t material to=
 the business, someone else would do it.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In a sense this means that any time you buy or=
 sell your company=E2=80=99s stock, you are =E2=80=9Cinsider trading,=E2=80=
=9D trading while in possession of material nonpublic information. There ar=
e solutions to this problem. One is the concept of the =E2=80=9Copen window=
=E2=80=9D:=C2=A0A public company will usually have a rule saying that its e=
mployees can only trade the stock in certain open-window periods shortly af=
ter the company releases earnings. The theory is that the earnings release =
=E2=80=94 and the earnings call that usually comes with it =E2=80=94 contai=
ns all of the material information about the company, so everything that ex=
ecutives know is now public, so the executives are free to trade.<a class=
=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-3" style=3D"color: #000000; text-deco=
ration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00=
0000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !=
important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Another important one, in the US,=C2=A0is the =
10b5-1 plan, named after a US Securities and Exchange Commission <a href=3D=
"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30772176.262407/aHR0cHM6Ly93=
d3cubGF3LmNvcm5lbGwuZWR1L2Nmci90ZXh0LzE3LzI0MC4xMGI1LTE/607f07ceef4b8524a31=
9a23cBb9b4a474" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_b=
lank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bot=
tom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; b=
ackground-color: #ccc;"> rule</a>. The idea in a 10b5-1 plan is that, when =
an executive does=C2=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>have material nonpublic informatio=
n,<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-4" style=3D"color: #000000;=
 text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-=
color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: tra=
nsparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a>=C2=A0she can enter into an automated plan with her broker telling the =
broker what trades to make in the future. You announce earnings, you wait a=
 day or two, then you sign a plan with your broker saying =E2=80=9Csell 10,=
000 shares per month for a year,=C2=A0starting next month.=E2=80=9D When yo=
ur broker actually sells those shares for you, you might=C2=A0<em>have=C2=
=A0</em>material nonpublic information, but you are not=C2=A0<em>trading=C2=
=A0</em>on that information: The sales are automated, and you signed up for=
 them before you learned any material nonpublic information.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There are ways to abuse this. A simple way is:=
 On Monday, you learn some really bad news that isn=E2=80=99t yet public; o=
n Tuesday,=C2=A0you sign a 10b5-1 plan saying =E2=80=9Csell everything tomo=
rrow=E2=80=9D;=C2=A0on Wednesday, the plan sells all your stock; and on Thu=
rsday you announce the bad news and the stock tanks. This one is boring. It=
 is not even really an abuse of Rule 10b5-1: This is just=C2=A0<em>insider =
trading</em>, and Rule 10b5-1 explicitly says that you have to enter the pl=
an =E2=80=9Cbefore becoming aware of the information=E2=80=9D for it to wor=
k. Still, lots of information is kind of ambiguous, and people do worry abo=
ut this sort of abuse.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In December, the SEC <a href=3D"https://link.m=
ail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30772176.262407/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9=
uZXdzL3ByZXNzLXJlbGVhc2UvMjAyMi0yMjI/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBe9ca31e6" ite=
mprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"colo=
r: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bor=
der-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #c=
cc;"> amended Rule 10b5-1</a> to address this concern by requiring a =E2=80=
=9Ccooling-off period=E2=80=9D: If a director or officer of a company signs=
 a=C2=A010b5-1 plan, it can=E2=80=99t actually trade any stock for 90 days.=
<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-5" style=3D"color: #000000; t=
ext-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-co=
lor: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: trans=
parent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[5]</span>
</a>=C2=A0The idea is that whether or not you know anything material=C2=A0<=
em>now, </em>it will probably be public or stale in three months, so you de=
finitely won=E2=80=99t be insider trading when your=C2=A0automatic trades s=
tart in three months.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A more interesting sort of abuse is: On Jan. 1=
, you have no material nonpublic information. But you know that on Feb. 15,=
 your company will report earnings; you don=E2=80=99t know what they will b=
e, but you know that the results are hotly anticipated and will be very imp=
ortant to the market. Also=C2=A0<em>you=C2=A0</em>will know the earnings by=
 Feb.=C2=A05, because you are a senior executive and will be involved in pr=
eparing the earnings release. So what you do is, you adopt a 10b5-1 plan, o=
n Jan. 1, telling your broker =E2=80=9Csell all of my stock over a five-day=
 period starting on Feb. 6.=E2=80=9D You have no material nonpublic informa=
tion and the plan doesn=E2=80=99t start for a month, so you look good. Then=
 on Feb. 5, you find out the earnings. If they are good, you call your brok=
er and=C2=A0<em>cancel the plan</em>: You have material nonpublic informati=
on, but you=C2=A0are not insider trading, because all you are doing is=C2=
=A0<em>deciding not to trade</em>. If the earnings are bad, you do nothing,=
 and your broker dumps all your stock before the earnings are announced: Th=
is is not insider trading, because the sales are all made under an automate=
d 10b5-1 plan.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This is also probably not allowed: Rule 10b5-1=
 says that you have to have entered into your plan =E2=80=9Cin good faith a=
nd not as part of a plan or scheme to evade the prohibitions of this sectio=
n,=E2=80=9D and this scheme pretty clearly looks like bad faith. In practic=
e it is not clear how you get=C2=A0<em>caught</em>, though: If you end up d=
oing the trade, then it looks like you were acting in good faith (you signe=
d a plan, and then followed it); if you end up not doing the trade, then yo=
u didn=E2=80=99t do any trades, so they can=E2=80=99t really get you for in=
sider trading.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-6" style=3D"col=
or: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bo=
rder-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; backgroun=
d-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[6]</span>
</a>=C2=A0(Not legal advice!)</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But last week the SEC <a href=3D"https://link.=
mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30772176.262407/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi=
9uZXdzL3ByZXNzLXJlbGVhc2UvMjAyMy00Mg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB4b9c527f" ite=
mprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"colo=
r: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bor=
der-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #c=
cc;">did bring an insider trading case</a> about alleged 10b5-1 abuse. Terr=
en Peizer was the executive chairman of Ontrak Inc., a behavioral-health-ca=
re company whose main customers are a handful of big health insurance compa=
nies. In March 2021, Ontrak announced that its largest customer (called =E2=
=80=9CCustomer B=E2=80=9D in <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness=
.com/click/30772176.262407/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9saXRpZ2F0aW9uL2NvbXBs=
YWludHMvMjAyMy9jb21wLXByMjAyMy00Mi5wZGY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBdf899a85" =
itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"c=
olor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; =
border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color:=
 #ccc;"> the SEC complaint</a>)=C2=A0was terminating its contract; the stoc=
k fell 46% on the day, and =E2=80=9CPeizer lost over $265 million in the va=
lue of his shares in a single day when Ontrak announced the loss of Custome=
r B.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">By late March, Ontrak=E2=80=99s next-largest c=
ustomer (called =E2=80=9CCustomer A=E2=80=9D in the complaint) was also =E2=
=80=9Cnot satisfied with Ontrak=E2=80=99s services and considering terminat=
ing its contract.=E2=80=9D Ontrak=E2=80=99s chief executive officer and its=
 president=C2=A0tried to salvage the relationship, and they kept Peizer =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0the executive chairman =E2=80=94=C2=A0up to speed:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Throughout April 2021, Peizer communicated wit=
h numerous individuals at Ontrak about the importance of saving the Custome=
r A relationship. For example, on April 3, 2021, Peizer texted an Ontrak co=
nsultant and close confidant: =E2=80=9CWe just need to save [Customer A] an=
d we are on our way.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On April 14, 2021, Peizer again texted the con=
sultant that Ontrak=E2=80=99s CEO =E2=80=9Cneeds to still save [Customer A]=
!=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The=C2=A0next day, Peizer texted Ontrak=E2=80=
=99s CEO: =E2=80=9CPlease just save [Customer A]=E2=80=A6then we will get b=
ack =E2=80=98Ontrak.=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I wonder how often he used that line. Anyway:<=
/p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">At the same time that Peizer was c=
ommunicating with others at Ontrak about saving the relationship with Custo=
mer A, he was pursuing a plan to sell shares of Ontrak securities. On May 3=
, 2021, Peizer contacted one of his brokerage firms (=E2=80=9CBroker 1=E2=
=80=9D) to set up a purported Rule 10b5-1 plan. Peizer explained in an emai=
l: =E2=80=9CI have 685,929 . . . warrants that expire August 15th-30th. I w=
ant to do a plan to sells 10,000 shares a day (~68 trading days). We report=
 our Q1 numbers Thursday after the close and our window opens up Friday aft=
er the close. My intent is to exercise the warrants, and file the plan.=E2=
=80=9D</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This was before the SEC amended Rule 10b5-1, s=
o there was no SEC-required cooling-off period. Peizer could have signed th=
e plan and started trading immediately, but his brokerage had its own 30-da=
y cooling-off period to avoid any risk of impropriety. They offered to go d=
own to 14 days, =E2=80=9Cbut having no cooling off is not negotiable.=E2=80=
=9D So he found another broker:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On May 4, 2021, Peizer texted a brokerage empl=
oyee at Broker 2, =E2=80=9CWhen you execute 10b5-1 plans, once the plan is =
filed, does [your brokerage firm] have a=C2=A0cooling off period?=E2=80=9D =
The employee responded, =E2=80=9CIt has been our experience that the coolin=
g off period is dictated by the company.=E2=80=9D Peizer replied, =E2=80=9C=
agreed.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Upon determining that Broker 2 did not require=
 a cooling off period, but would allow him to start selling stock immediate=
ly after establishing a Rule 10b5-1 plan, Peizer moved forward in setting u=
p a purported Rule 10b5-1 plan at Broker 2. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Specifically, the May Trading Plan instructed =
Broker 2 to sell 11,000 shares of Ontrak common stock per day at the volume=
 weighted average price (=E2=80=9CVWAP=E2=80=9D) (or more shares per day at=
 specified limit prices), starting on May 11, 2021=E2=80=94the first busine=
ss day after the May Trading Plan was established.=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The timing was pretty good:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On a May 18, 2021 call, Customer A notified On=
trak=E2=80=99s CEO of its intention to terminate its contract effective Dec=
ember 31, 2021.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That same day, Ontrak=E2=80=99s CEO notified P=
eizer via text message: =E2=80=9CCustomer A is intending to end relationshi=
p at the end of the year 12-31-21=E2=80=A6.They are really firm with me. <s=
trong>Decision has been made</strong>.=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Nonetheless, Ontrak kept trying to save the re=
lationship, and did not=C2=A0<em>announce </em>Customer A=E2=80=99s termina=
tion, so the stock stayed up while Peizer=E2=80=99s 10b5-1 plan was selling=
. On Aug. 13, he did a new 10b5-1 plan:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Specifically, the August Trading Plan instruct=
ed Broker 2 to sell 11,000 shares of Ontrak common stock per day at the VWA=
P (or more shares per day at specified limit prices), starting the next tra=
ding day, August 16, 2021.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In an internal email between Broker 2 employee=
s, one stated that he =E2=80=9Calways recommend[s] 30 day cooling off, but =
for Terren [Peizer] that won=E2=80=99t work.=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Meanwhile:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On August 13, 2021=E2=80=94the sam=
e day that Peizer set up a second Rule 10b5-1 trading plan to sell Ontrak s=
tock, as discussed below=E2=80=94Peizer called the Ontrak Sales Employee an=
d asked if Customer A was going to terminate the contract. The Ontrak Sales=
 Employee indicated to Peizer that he thought Customer A would terminate.</=
p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The timing here was less good: On Aug. 18, Cus=
tomer A sent Ontrak a formal termination, and on Aug. 19 Ontrak <a href=3D"=
https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/30772176.262407/aHR0cHM6Ly93d=
3cuc2VjLmdvdi9peD9kb2M9L0FyY2hpdmVzL2VkZ2FyL2RhdGEvMTEzNjE3NC8wMDAxNjI4Mjgw=
MjEwMTcyODQvY2F0cy0yMDIxMDgxOC5odG0/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBcfecf471" item=
prop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color=
: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bord=
er-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #cc=
c;"> announced the termination publicly</a>. The stock traded down 44% that=
 day, closing at $11.68.=C2=A0Ontrak closed yesterday=C2=A0at $0.56, for a =
market capitalization of about $15 million. Turns out losing all your bigge=
st customers is bad. Peizer sold $19.2 million of stock under his first pla=
n and $1.9 million under his second (in the couple of days before the termi=
nation was announced); the SEC says that was insider trading.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There are, I think, three ways to tell this st=
ory:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Peizer was involved in the business, =
and like any senior executive he knew a bunch of stuff, there was a lot goi=
ng on, and nothing was certain. Ontrak was constantly negotiating with cust=
omers and getting feedback; that is just part of the job. In May, he decide=
d to sell some stock from some options that were expiring, so he signed a d=
eal to do that. He had no =E2=80=9Cmaterial nonpublic information=E2=80=9D:=
 He didn=E2=80=99t know that a big cancellation was coming; he just knew th=
at the business was hard and risky and some customers were complaining. (It=
=E2=80=99s harder to defend the August trades, but I suppose they were just=
 a continuation of the May ones?)</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">=
Peizer had material nonpublic information, in May and in August, that Custo=
mer A was planning to cancel. So he decided to sell stock as quickly as pos=
sible, but put the fig leaf of a 10b5-1 plan over his sales. But 10b5-1 is =
not a defense if you knew the material nonpublic information when you signe=
d the plan; the fig leaf doesn=E2=80=99t work.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-b=
ottom: 5px;">Peizer kinda knew stuff was going south, but he wanted to give=
 it time to play out. So he signed a plan to start dumping stock, one that =
he could have canceled if things improved with Customer A. But they never d=
id, so he kept selling. He didn=E2=80=99t know anything material and bad wh=
en he signed the May plan, but he signed the May plan to give himself some =
optionality to sell if he learned anything material and bad, and that itsel=
f is a kind of insider trading.</li>
</ol>
